n_1.text:
Dawn comes grey and heavy. You stand on the Adamant's quarterdeck and watch Nassau wake — smoke rising from cook-fires, the distant clang of the shipwright's hammer, gulls working the shallows. The fourth Crown ship sits where it anchored yesterday, completing the line. Four warships. The harbour mouth is a needle's eye now.

Pell comes up from below, wiping his hands on a rag. "Crew's stowing the last of the water casks. We can make the tide if we leave by noon." He pauses. "But there's time, Captain. A few hours. If there's business ashore."

You have Morrow's chart. You have the location. What you do with the hours between now and the tide is your decision.

choices.text: Go ashore — there are questions that need answers
Stay aboard and prepare the crew

n_2.text:
Nassau's streets are quieter than yesterday. The blockade is doing its work — fewer ships in the harbour means fewer sailors spending coin, and the market stalls show it. Half the fruit sellers haven't bothered to set up. A dog works a fishbone in the gutter.

The Broken Wheel is closed at this hour, shutters drawn. The harbour office is open, a clerk visible through the window. The path toward the Council house is empty. And somewhere in this town, there are answers about why Reyna Morrow sat on the San Cristóbal's location for three years.

choices.text: Visit the harbour office — check the shipping records
Find Drest — he knew the San Cristóbal's captain
Go to the Council house — speak with Crane
Check the Broken Wheel — someone may be talking
Walk the docks — check on the Adamant's supplies
Ask Morrow directly why she waited three years
Deal with Morrow's enforcer

n_3.text:
The harbour clerk is a thin man named Pryce who smells of ink and tallow. Crane's letter of marque gets you through the door. Pryce sets out the ledgers without enthusiasm — three years of shipping records, bound in salt-stained canvas.

You tell him you're looking for Morrow's movements, three years back. He doesn't ask why. He finds the volume and opens it to the right quarter.

The entries are sparse but clear. Morrow's ship, the Tempest, made a southern run in April of that year. Destination listed as "general trading, southern cays." No cargo manifest filed on return. One crewman — a diver named Collis — was paid off after the voyage and never appeared in Nassau records again.

choices.text: Ask Pryce about the missing cargo manifest
Ask about the diver, Collis
Take this information and leave

n_4.text:
Pryce shrugs. "Captains skip the manifest when they're carrying nothing worth declaring. Or when they're carrying something they don't want declared." He taps the ledger. "Morrow files every manifest. Every voyage. Except this one."

He leans back. "She paid the late filing fee without complaint. Fifty crowns. Most captains would argue it down to ten. She paid it the day she docked and never mentioned the voyage again."

Fifty crowns to avoid questions. That's the price of a secret in Nassau.

choices.text: Ask about the diver, Collis
You have enough — leave the harbour office

n_5.text:
Pryce checks a separate ledger — crew registrations. "Collis. Free diver, registered out of Port Royal originally. Signed aboard the Tempest as specialist crew for one voyage." He runs his finger down the column. "Paid off at Nassau on return. Full share plus a bonus — three times the standard rate."

He looks up. "After that, nothing. No other ship took him on. No departure record. He either left Nassau on foot, died quietly, or someone made sure he wasn't available for questions."

The pattern is clear. Morrow hired a diver, took the Tempest south, filed no cargo manifest, paid the diver triple rate, and the diver vanished. Whatever she found on that voyage, she found it at the bottom of the sea.

choices.text: You have enough — leave the harbour office

n_6.text:
You step out of the harbour office into the morning light. The pieces are taking shape — Morrow's southern run, the missing manifest, the diver who disappeared. She went to the San Cristóbal three years ago. She recovered something. And she has been sitting on it ever since.

The question is what she found, and why she kept it to herself.

choices.text: Find Drest — he might know more
Confront Morrow with what you've found
Confront Morrow's people with what you've found
Return to the Adamant

n_7.text:
You find Drest where you found him before — at the harbour wall, mending a net that will never be finished. He's been mending it for years. It gives him something to do with his hands while he watches the harbour and remembers the ships he used to sail on.

He sees you coming and doesn't look up. "Back again, Captain. Still asking questions about dead ships and dead men."

choices.text: Ask about Morrow's southern run three years ago
Ask about the diver named Collis
Ask what he knows about the Red Correspondence

n_8.text:
Drest's hands slow on the net. "Three years back. Aye." He's quiet for a long time. "The Tempest left harbour on a Tuesday. I remember because Morrow never sails on Tuesdays — says they're bad luck. But she sailed that Tuesday."

He picks at a knot. "She came back four days later. Quiet about it. Didn't celebrate, didn't complain. But she was different after. Steadier. Like someone who's found something that changes the arithmetic." He looks at you with those rheumy eyes. "A captain who stops being afraid of the blockade — that's a captain who found leverage somewhere."

choices.text: Ask about the diver named Collis
Ask what he knows about the Red Correspondence
You've heard enough from Drest

n_9.text:
"Collis." Drest says the name like he's tasting something sour. "Good diver. Best I've seen — could hold his breath three minutes, work a wreck in current that would kill most men. Morrow hired him special for that one voyage."

He sets the net down. "I saw Collis the night they came back. He was drinking alone at the Wheel, the kind of drinking a man does when he's seen something he can't talk about. I bought him a rum. He said one thing: 'She took one piece and left the rest.' Then he finished his drink and I never saw him again."

One piece. From a wreck full of gold and secrets, Morrow took one piece and left everything else on the ocean floor.

choices.text: Ask what he knows about the Red Correspondence
You've heard enough from Drest

n_10.text:
Drest goes very still. "The Red Correspondence. You know about that." It's not a question.

"Captain Herrera — the San Cristóbal's master — he carried a diplomatic pouch sealed in red wax. Crown cipher. Herrera told me once, when we were drunk enough to say true things, that the pouch contained letters that could end careers in London. Names, dates, payments. The kind of thing that makes admirals sweat."

He looks out at the blockade. "If Morrow has even one of those letters — one page — she has something Ashford would kill to get back. And Ashford knows it. That's why he sits out there. That's why he waits. He's not blockading Nassau. He's blockading Morrow."

choices.text: Confront Morrow with what you've found
Return to the Adamant — you need to think

n_11.text:
You find Morrow at the Tempest's stern rail, watching the harbour with a cup of coffee going cold in her hand. She sees your expression and sets the cup down.

"You've been asking questions," she says. Not angry. Not surprised. The way someone acknowledges the weather.

You tell her what you've pieced together. The southern run. Collis the diver. The missing manifest. The one piece she took from the San Cristóbal and the rest she left behind.

Morrow is quiet for a long time. When she speaks, her voice is level. "Three years ago, I sent Collis down to confirm the wreck's position. He found the captain's cabin. He brought up one item — a single letter from the Red Correspondence. A partial copy, not the full pouch."

She meets your eyes. "I've been using that letter to keep Ashford at arm's length. Personal protection. Not Nassau's defence. Mine."

choices.text: "I need you to help me — or I use this."
"Take this to Crane. Nassau deserves to know."
"I'm not going to use this. But I needed you to know that I know."

n_12.text:
Morrow goes still. Not the stillness of someone thinking — the stillness of someone who has already finished thinking and arrived at a place they expected to reach.

"You want my ship at the Shallows," she says.

"I want your ship. Your crew. Your knowledge of the wreck site. And I want you to stay out of the Red Correspondence when we recover it."

She looks at the harbour. At the four Crown ships. At Nassau, which she has protected only incidentally while protecting herself. Then she looks at you.

"Agreed." One word. Cold and precise as a contract clause. She will honour the deal. She will not forgive it, and she will not forget it. You can see both of those facts in her expression as clearly as if she had written them down.

"The Tempest sails with the tide. I'll be at the Shallows ahead of you."

choices.text: Leave the Tempest

n_13.text:
Morrow's expression doesn't change. She watches you stand, watches you turn toward the gangplank, and says nothing. There is nothing to say. You both know what happens next.

You find Crane at the Council house. He reads your account with the particular attention of a man who has suspected something for a long time and is now seeing confirmation. He asks three precise questions. You answer them.

"This changes things," Crane says, folding his hands. "Morrow's position in Nassau was built on the assumption that she was acting in the port's interest. If she has been holding Crown leverage for personal use—" He pauses. "The Council will want to hear this. Formally."

Morrow's political position in Nassau collapses in the time it takes Crane to draft a memorandum. She will know by midday. She will know who told him.

choices.text: Leave the Council house

n_14.text:
You find Morrow at the Tempest, as you expected. She is alone on the quarterdeck — Harlow and the crew are below. She sees you and nods once.

"You want to know why I sat on the location for three years."

You don't answer. You don't need to. She invited you here. She knows.

Morrow leans against the rail and looks at the harbour. "I found the wreck three years ago. I sent a diver down — a man named Collis. He brought up one item from the captain's cabin. A letter. One page of the Red Correspondence." She pauses. "Not the full pouch. A partial copy."

Her voice is steady and unadorned. "I've been using that letter to keep Ashford at bay. Not for Nassau. For myself. Nassau has been unprotected this entire time, and I let that happen because the alternative was giving up the one card I held."

She turns to face you. "That's why. No noble reason. No grand strategy. I chose myself."

choices.text: "I need you to help me — or I use this."
"Take this to Crane. Nassau deserves to know."
"I'm not going to use this. But I needed you to know that I know."

n_15.text:
The enforcer is waiting for you in the alley behind the Broken Wheel — a broad man with a scarred jaw and patient eyes. He steps out of the shadow like he was born there.

"Captain Morrow wants a word about your questions," he says. His hand rests on a belaying pin at his belt. "She'd prefer you stopped asking them."

choices.text: "Tell Morrow I'll ask what I like."
"I already have my answers. Take me to her."
Walk away — you don't need this fight

n_16.text: The enforcer doesn't argue. He draws the belaying pin and comes at you with the economical violence of a man who has done this many times before.

n_17.text:
The enforcer studies you for a moment, then nods. "This way."

He leads you through the back streets to the Tempest's berth. Morrow is on the quarterdeck. She sees your expression — the expression of someone who has pieced together enough — and dismisses the enforcer with a gesture.

"You know," she says. Flat. Certain.

"I know you sent a diver to the San Cristóbal three years ago. I know he brought something up. I know you've been using it to keep Ashford away from you — not from Nassau. From you."

Morrow is silent for a ten-count. Then: "A letter. One page of the Red Correspondence. A partial copy from the captain's cabin." She says it the way someone lances a wound — deliberate, necessary, painful. "I kept it for myself. Nassau was never part of the calculation."

choices.text: "I need you to help me — or I use this."
"Take this to Crane. Nassau deserves to know."
"I'm not going to use this. But I needed you to know that I know."

n_17a.text:
The enforcer drops to one knee, bleeding from a cut above his ear. He looks up at you with more respect than anger. "You've made your point, Captain."

You haul him to his feet. "Take me to Morrow. Now."

He nods, pressing a rag to his head, and leads you to the Tempest. Morrow watches you come aboard with an expression that is difficult to read — not surprise, not anger, something closer to reassessment.

"Your man tried to discourage me," you say. "It didn't take."

"I can see that." She looks at the enforcer, then back at you. "You've been asking questions about my southern run. The diver. The missing manifest." She pauses. "Ask your question, Captain. The real one."

So you do. And she answers. A letter — one page of the Red Correspondence, recovered from the San Cristóbal's cabin three years ago. Used for personal leverage against Ashford. Not for Nassau. For herself.

choices.text: "I need you to help me — or I use this."
"Take this to Crane. Nassau deserves to know."
"I'm not going to use this. But I needed you to know that I know."

n_18.text:
A long silence. The harbour sounds fill it — rigging, gulls, the slap of water against hulls. Morrow stands at the rail and does not look at you. She is doing arithmetic in her head, recalculating everything, and you let her.

When she speaks, her voice is quiet. Not soft — Morrow is never soft — but quiet in the way of someone who has set down something heavy.

"I'll be at the Shallows."

Nothing more. She does not explain. She does not thank you. She turns back to the harbour and picks up her cold coffee, and the conversation is over. You walk down the gangplank and into the Nassau morning, and whatever passed between you on that quarterdeck stays there.

choices.text: Return to the docks

n_19.text:
The morning is burning off. The harbour is busier now — longboats moving between ships, a fishing sloop trying to work the shallows inside the blockade line. The Crown ships sit in their formation, patient as gravestones.

You have time before the tide. The Adamant needs attention, and so does her crew.

choices.text: Walk the docks — check the Adamant's supplies
Go to the Council house — speak with Crane
Cut through the back streets to the Adamant
Return to the Adamant by the main road

n_20.text:
The Council house is a converted warehouse — whitewashed walls, a long table, maps pinned to every surface. Crane is here, as always, dressed too well for the climate, writing in a ledger with precise, small handwriting.

He looks up when you enter. "Captain. I expected you'd come." He sets down his pen. "The fourth ship changes things. Ashford isn't posturing anymore — he's building a cage. Nassau has weeks, not months."

choices.text: Ask Crane about Ashford's patrol patterns near the Shallows
Ask about the harbour records — Morrow's southern run
Sign Crane's letter of marque
Tell Crane about Ashford's tightening blockade
Leave the Council house

n_21.text:
Crane opens a chart locker and spreads a map across the table. "The Shallows of Perdition. Ashford runs a patrol through here every six days — the Resolve, usually, sometimes the Fervent." He marks the patrol route with a pencil. "The passage is narrow. If you time your approach for the gap between patrols, you'll have roughly eighteen hours on site before the next circuit."

He taps the chart. "But with the fourth ship, he may shorten the cycle. I'd plan for twelve hours to be safe."

This is the kind of intelligence that wins or loses expeditions. Crane offers it freely — because you signed his letter, because your success is Nassau's success. The arithmetic is clean.

choices.text: Ask about the harbour records — Morrow's southern run
Thank Crane and leave

n_22.text:
Crane pulls a bound ledger from the shelf without hesitation — he knows exactly which one you need. "Morrow's Tempest. April, three years ago. Southern run, no cargo manifest, one specialist crewman paid off on return." He opens to the page.

"I noticed the irregularity at the time," he says. "Morrow has always been meticulous about her filings. One missing manifest in fifteen years of trading — that's a signal, not an oversight."

He closes the ledger. "I didn't press the matter. Morrow was useful to Nassau, and useful people are allowed their secrets. But if what she found on that voyage is what I think she found—" He lets the sentence hang.

choices.text: Tell Crane what Morrow found
Keep Morrow's secret for now

n_23.text:
Crane produces the document from a drawer — he has been keeping it ready. The letter of marque is written on heavy paper, the Council's seal pressed in wax at the bottom. It commits the Adamant to Nassau's defence in exchange for harbour rights, provisioning priority, and the Council's political backing.

"This isn't charity," Crane says. "It's investment. Nassau needs captains who will fight for the port, not just from it." He offers you the pen.

You sign. The ink dries quickly in the heat. Crane folds the letter carefully and files his copy.

"Good," he says. "Now we're aligned. What do you need?"

choices.text: Ask Crane about Ashford's patrol patterns near the Shallows
Thank Crane and leave

n_24.text:
Crane listens without interrupting — a rare quality. When you finish, he nods once.

"Four ships. Ashford has been asking London for that fourth ship for eight months. Now he has it, he'll move within the month. Maybe sooner." Crane folds his hands. "Whatever you're planning at the Shallows, Captain, do it quickly. Nassau's window is closing."

He pauses. "The Council can help. If you're willing to commit to Nassau formally, I have a letter of marque with your name on it. Resources, intelligence, political cover. In exchange for your loyalty to this port."

choices.text: Sign Crane's letter of marque
"I'll think about it."
Ask about Ashford's patrol patterns

n_25.text:
A boy appears at your elbow — twelve, maybe thirteen, barefoot, with the careful blankness of someone who has been told to deliver a message and nothing else.

"For you, Captain." He holds out a folded paper, sealed with plain wax. No mark. No name.

You take it. The boy disappears into the crowd before you can ask who sent him.

The seal breaks cleanly. Inside, a single sheet in a careful, educated hand:

"Captain — I represent interests aboard the Crown's Justice. My principal offers safe passage through the blockade for the Adamant, no questions, no pursuit. In exchange: the Red Correspondence, delivered before it is read by any other party. The gold from the war chest is yours to keep. Reply by sunset. Leave your answer with the boy at the east dock stairs. — A friend."

choices.text: Study the handwriting carefully
Write a reply — safe passage is worth considering
Burn the note and tell no one

n_26.text:
You unfold the marine's notebook and compare the handwriting. The cipher marks in the margin — small crosses and half-circles — match exactly. This is Ashford's system. You've seen it in the surveillance records: the same shorthand, the same precise letter formation.

This is no trick. No intermediary guessing at an opportunity. This comes from the Commodore himself.

Ashford is offering you a deal. The Correspondence for safe passage. He wants those letters badly enough to let a pirate sail free.

choices.text: Write a reply — safe passage is worth considering
Burn the note and tell no one

n_27.text:
You write a single line on the back of the note: "Terms received. I will consider them."

Noncommittal. It opens a door without walking through it. You fold the paper, seal it with a drop of candle wax, and leave it with the barefoot boy at the east dock stairs. He takes it without a word and vanishes.

The deal is not accepted. But the channel is open. Ashford knows you're willing to talk, and that knowledge is itself a kind of currency — one that can be spent later, at the Shallows, when the stakes are higher.

choices.text: Continue

n_28.text:
You hold the note over the nearest lantern and watch it curl and blacken. The wax seal pops in the heat. Ashford's careful handwriting turns to ash and drifts into the harbour.

No deal. No channel. Whatever happens at the Shallows, you will face the Crown as an enemy, not a negotiating partner. There is a clarity in that, if nothing else.

You brush the ash from your fingers and walk on.

choices.text: Continue

n_29.text:
The morning is nearly gone. The sun is high and the harbour bakes in the heat. Across the water, the Crown ships sit in their line, unmoved, unmoving. Whatever reply you sent — or didn't — the blockade remains.

The tide turns soon. The Adamant waits.

choices.text: Return to the Adamant along the harbour front
One more stop before sailing — walk the docks

n_30.text:
The docks are busy with the low commerce of a port under siege — repairs, not refitting; mending, not building. A chandler's stall sells rope at twice last month's price. A sail-maker works a patch onto canvas that should have been replaced years ago.

The Adamant sits in her berth, riding low with fresh water casks. She looks ready. Your crew looks less certain.

choices.text: Buy extra supplies from the chandler
Talk to the other captains about the Shallows
Find a pilot who knows the reef channels
Check the Adamant's hold
Look south toward the Shallows from the harbour wall
Speak with your crew

n_31.text:
The chandler is a squat woman named Gerda who has been selling rope and tar to Nassau's captains for twenty years. She looks at your list and shakes her head.

"Extra rope, extra chain, grappling hooks. You're going to a wreck." She doesn't ask which one. "Forty crowns for the lot. Blockade prices."

You pay. She hands over the supplies with the efficiency of someone who expects to be the last person selling anything in this harbour. "Whatever you're after, Captain, bring it back. This port needs a win."

choices.text: Talk to the other captains about the Shallows
Find a pilot who knows the reef channels
Speak with your crew
Return to the Adamant and prepare to sail

n_32.text:
You find Cork at the far end of the dock, sitting on a bollard and smoking a pipe. He sailed aboard the Gallant for years — a ship that worked the southern cays before the blockade closed everything down.

"The Shallows of Perdition." Cork takes the pipe from his mouth. "Sailed past them twice. Wouldn't go in for any money." He spits into the harbour. "The reef comes up fast — one fathom of water where the chart says five. Currents run crosswise. And the bottom is sharp enough to open a hull like a tin."

He draws a rough diagram on the dock planking with a piece of chalk. "There's a channel on the eastern approach — narrow, but deep enough for a sloop. The Gallant's pilot used it once. Name of Sable. She's still in Nassau, if she hasn't drunk herself to death."

choices.text: Ask Cork about the San Cristóbal
Find this pilot — Sable
Return to the Adamant

n_33.text:
Cork's expression changes. "The San Cristóbal. Crown treasury galleon. She went down in a storm — drove straight onto the reef. The Crown spent two years looking for her and never found the wreck." He taps out his pipe. "Heavy ship. Iron-bound chests in the hold. She'll have settled deep into the coral by now."

He looks at you sidelong. "If someone had a chart of where she lies, and a crew willing to dive on her — they'd need to be fast. The reef shifts. What's accessible today might be buried next year." He pauses. "And whatever's in that wreck, the Crown wants it back. Badly. That's not a rumour. That's a fact."

Cork knows more than he's saying. But he's said enough. The San Cristóbal is real, it's reachable, and the clock is ticking on the coral as surely as it's ticking on the blockade.

choices.text: Find the pilot — Sable
Return to the Adamant

n_34.text:
You find Sable in a boarding house two streets back from the harbour — a lean woman with sun-darkened skin and eyes that have spent too long reading water. She's sober, barely, and unimpressed by your arrival.

"I know the eastern channel into the Shallows," she says when you explain what you need. "Piloted the Gallant through it in forty-three. Lost a yard of keel to the coral, but we made it."

She names her price: passage out of Nassau and a full share of whatever you recover. The blockade has trapped her here. She wants out.

choices.text: Agree to her terms — you need a pilot
Decline — you'll navigate the channel yourself

n_35.text:
Sable nods once. "I'll be at your gangplank within the hour." She's already packing — a sea bag and a leather case of charts, nothing else. A life reduced to what fits in two bags.

At the door she pauses. "Captain. The Shallows are honest. The reef doesn't lie and the current doesn't negotiate. If you listen to me when I tell you to turn, we'll be fine. If you don't, the coral will do what it's done to better ships than yours."

She leaves. You have a pilot. The Adamant's chances just improved considerably.

choices.text: Speak with your crew before sailing
Return to the Adamant and prepare to sail

n_35a.text:
Sable shrugs. "Your funeral, Captain. The eastern channel has three turns that don't show on any chart. Miss one and the reef takes your keel." She sits back down. "I'll be here when you change your mind. If you come back."

You leave the boarding house. You have Morrow's chart, your own navigation skills, and the Adamant's shallow draught. It will have to be enough.

choices.text: Speak with your crew before sailing
Return to the Adamant and prepare to sail

n_36.text:
The crew is gathered on the main deck, making busy with the kind of work that men do when they're waiting and want to look like they're not. Pell stands by the mainmast, watching you come aboard.

You call them together. Fourteen men and women. They know something is happening — they've watched you going back and forth in Nassau all morning. They don't know what.

"We're sailing south," you say. "To the Shallows of Perdition. There's a wreck — a Crown treasury galleon called the San Cristóbal. Gold, diplomatic papers. The kind of haul that changes everything."

Silence. They're measuring the words against the blockade sitting in the harbour mouth.

choices.text: Tell them the truth — the risks are real, but so is the prize
Tell them only what they need to know

n_37.text:
"I'm not going to dress this up," you say. "The Shallows will tear the bottom out of any ship that isn't careful. The Crown wants what's in that wreck — enough to blockade an entire port for it. And we're going to take it from under their noses."

You look at them — each face, each pair of eyes. "Anyone who wants off can go ashore now, full pay, no questions. I won't hold it against you."

No one moves. Pell catches your eye and gives a small nod. The crew trusts you. Not because of a speech — because of how you've carried yourself since you arrived in Nassau. Decisions accumulate. This is what they add up to.

choices.text: The crew is ready. Prepare to sail.

n_38.text:
"There's a prize worth taking south of here. Gold. More than any of us have seen." You keep the details sparse. The Red Correspondence, Morrow's secret, the political weight of what you're after — none of that is their concern. "We sail with the tide. Extra pay for every hand who stays aboard."

They accept it. Sailors follow orders; it's the contract. But Pell lingers after the others disperse.

"They'll do it," he says quietly. "But they'll do it better if they know why. Sailors aren't fools, Captain. They know when they're being managed."

He's right. But you've made your choice.

choices.text: Prepare to sail

n_39.text:
You walk the harbour front one last time. Nassau in the late morning — the smell of cooking fires and tar, the distant sound of a hammer on iron, a woman singing in one of the houses above the waterline. A town that doesn't know if it will exist in six months.

A group of children run past, barefoot, chasing a dog. An old man sits outside the chandlery, carving a piece of driftwood into something that might be a bird. The mundane machinery of a place that is trying very hard to be ordinary while four warships sit in its harbour mouth.

This is what you're sailing for. Not the gold — or not only the gold. This place, these people, this stubborn experiment in living outside the Crown's permission.

choices.text: Return to the Adamant

n_40.text:
You step aboard the Adamant and the ship feels different — taut, ready, alive with purpose. Pell has the crew working in that efficient silence that means every man knows his job and is doing it.

The tide will turn within the hour. It's time.

But the harbour is not finished with you yet.

choices.text: Continue preparations
Inspect the hull from the waterline

n_41.text:
Pell appears at your side. "Captain. There's a boat coming alongside. Small craft, one man rowing." He frowns. "He's coming from the direction of the Crown's Justice."

You go to the rail. A harbour skiff, battered and salt-stained, with a single figure pulling at the oars. He's dressed like a fisherman — but fishermen don't row toward pirate ships with that kind of deliberate calm.

The man ties off at the Adamant's side and looks up. "Captain. A word, if you please. In private."

choices.text: Let him aboard — hear what he has to say
Let him aboard — you know what this is about
"No private words. Say it from the boat or leave."

n_42.text:
You've already received Ashford's message. This man has nothing new to offer.

"Tell the Commodore his terms were received," you say from the rail. "I've given my answer. There's nothing else to discuss."

The man studies you for a moment, then nods. He unties the skiff and rows back toward the blockade line without another word. Professional. Ashford's people always are.

Pell watches him go. "Crown business?" You don't answer, and Pell doesn't press. He knows when not to ask.

choices.text: Continue preparations for departure

n_42a.text:
The man in the skiff looks at you, then at the crew watching from the rail. He reaches into his coat and holds up a folded paper — sealed with plain wax.

"From a mutual friend, Captain. I was told to deliver it personally." He sets it on the Adamant's gunwale and rows away without waiting for a reply.

You take the note. Plain wax, no mark. Inside, a single sheet.

choices.text: Read the note

n_43.text:
The sun is past its peak. The tide is turning — you can feel it in the way the Adamant shifts at her mooring, the harbour current reversing direction. The water runs outward now, toward the sea, toward the blockade, toward the Shallows of Perdition.

Pell appears. "Ready to cast off, Captain. Crew at stations. Water and provisions stowed." He pauses. "Course, south-southeast?"

"South-southeast," you confirm. "The Shallows of Perdition."

choices.text: Cast off

n_44.text:
The mooring lines come in. The Adamant drifts free of the dock, catching the outgoing tide, her bow swinging toward the harbour mouth. Canvas drops and fills — the evening wind, right on schedule, pushing south.

Nassau slides past to port. The fort. The rooftops. The long curve of the harbour wall where Drest sits with his endless net. The Broken Wheel, shuttered now against the afternoon heat. A town holding its breath.

Four Crown warships sit at the harbour mouth. The Adamant will have to thread between them.

choices.text: Take one last look at Nassau
Take the helm

n_45.text:
You stand at the tiller as the Adamant makes for the harbour mouth. The Crown's Justice is the closest — a third-rate, sixty guns, her hull a wall of oak and iron. You can see figures on her deck watching you through glasses.

The gap between the Justice and the Fervent is narrow but passable. Ashford is blockading the harbour, not sealing it — he wants to control traffic, not stop it entirely. A single sloop, sailing south, is not worth breaking the blockade's discipline.

Not yet.

test_luck.lucky_text: The Adamant slides through the gap like a knife through water. The Crown ships don't react — you pass close enough to see the marine sentries on the Justice's quarterdeck, and none of them raises an alarm. Clean passage. The open sea opens ahead.
test_luck.unlucky_text: A signal gun fires from the Fervent — a warning shot, not an attack. A longboat launches from the Resolve, pulling hard toward your position. They want to inspect you. You crowd on sail and the Adamant surges forward, but the delay costs you time and nerve.

n_46.text:
The longboat falls behind as the Adamant clears the harbour mouth and hits the open swell. The Crown ships make no further move — they are not going to chase a sloop. Not today. But the signal flags run up the Justice's mast, and you know what that means: Ashford has noted your departure. He knows which direction you're heading.

The clock has started. Whatever time you had at the Shallows just got shorter.

Pell comes aft. "They'll send word to their patrol. We'll have company at the Shallows sooner than we'd like."

He's right. But you're clear of the harbour. Nassau falls behind, growing smaller on the horizon. Ahead, nothing but sea and the long run south.

choices.text: Set course for the Shallows

n_47.text:
The harbour mouth falls behind. The Crown ships recede, shrinking to silhouettes against the afternoon sky. No pursuit. No signal guns. Just the open sea, the wind filling the Adamant's canvas, and the long coastline of the Bahamas stretching south.

Pell lets out a breath he's been holding since the first Crown ship came abeam. "Clean exit. Better than I expected."

Nassau disappears behind the headland — the fort, the harbour, the rooftops, all of it folding into the coastline until it's just another stretch of green and sand. A town you might never see again, depending on what happens next.

choices.text: Set course for the Shallows

n_48.text:
You're three streets from the docks when they come at you. Two men, moving fast from a side alley — one with a cudgel, one with a knife. Not robbers. Too purposeful, too coordinated. Someone sent them.

The one with the knife grins. "Captain Briggs sends his regards. That wreck is his claim, and you're not sailing anywhere near it."

n_49.text:
The last of them goes down, clutching a cut arm. You stand over him, breathing hard.

"Briggs," you say. "Tell me about Briggs."

The man talks — he's in no position not to. Briggs is a minor Nassau captain who tried to run the blockade a week ago and was turned back by the Fervent. He's desperate, broke, and he's heard the rumours about the San Cristóbal. He hired these men to discourage you — or kill you, whichever proved easier.

"He's got no ship, no crew, and no chart," the man says, spitting blood. "But he's got nothing to lose, either. He'll try again."

Briggs. A desperate man with nothing to lose. You file the name away and leave the alley.

choices.text: Continue to the Adamant

n_49a.text:
You break free and run — through the alley, across the market square, into the crowd. They don't follow. They've made their point, or tried to.

You catch your breath by the harbour wall. Someone in Nassau wants you away from the San Cristóbal badly enough to hire muscle. A captain named Briggs — you heard the name before they came at you. A minor player, desperate enough to be dangerous.

The Adamant is close. Time to get aboard and stay aboard.

choices.text: Get back to the Adamant

n_50.text:
The Adamant settles into her course — south-southeast, the wind on her quarter, the hull cutting through a low swell. The crew works the rigging with practised ease. Whatever doubts they had in harbour, the open sea has dissolved them. Sailors are always better once they're sailing.

Pell brings the chart to the quarterdeck. Morrow's annotations are precise — depth soundings, current patterns, the narrow approach to the reef. The San Cristóbal's position is marked with a small red cross, thirty fathoms down in a channel between two coral heads.

"Six hours to the Shallows at this speed," Pell says. "We'll arrive at dusk."

choices.text: Review the chart with Pell
Walk the deck — check on the crew
Enjoy the passage south

n_51.text:
You study the chart together. The Shallows of Perdition earned their name — a maze of coral reef, submerged rock, and unpredictable current spread across twenty square miles of shallow water. Ships have been dying here for a hundred years.

Morrow's chart marks a safe approach from the east — the channel Cork mentioned, the one Sable piloted the Gallant through. It's narrow, but it's deep enough for the Adamant.

"Once we're inside the reef," Pell says, "we'll be sheltered from any ship that doesn't know the channel. That's our advantage. Crown patrols won't follow us in — they draw too much water."

He taps the red cross. "The San Cristóbal. Thirty fathoms. We'll need divers and time. Both are in short supply."

choices.text: Ask Pell about the dive plan
Walk the deck

n_52.text:
"Tomás is our best diver," Pell says. "He can work at thirty fathoms for three minutes, maybe four. We'll need weighted lines, a signal system, and calm water." He looks at the sky. "Weather's holding. If it stays clear, we'll have two days before the next blow."

He marks the sequence on the chart. "First dive: confirm the wreck's position. Second: assess the hull — where she's opened up, where we can get in. Third and after: recovery. The captain's cabin and the cargo hold."

It's a good plan. Methodical. The kind of plan that works when nothing goes wrong. You've been at sea long enough to know that something always goes wrong.

choices.text: Walk the deck — check on the crew

n_53.text:
The sun drops toward the horizon. The sea turns copper, then gold, then a deep burning orange that silhouettes the Adamant's rigging against the sky. The wind holds steady. The ship runs south.

You stand at the stern rail and watch Nassau's coast disappear behind you. Somewhere back there, Crane is calculating odds. Somewhere back there, Ashford is reading reports. Somewhere back there, Morrow is making her own decision about whether to follow.

Ahead, the water changes colour — lighter, greener, the first sign of shallow ground. The Shallows of Perdition. The place where the San Cristóbal went down with her secrets and her gold, and where everything that happens next will be decided.

choices.text: Check the horizon before the light fails
Try to rest before dawn

n_54.text:
Night falls on the open water. The Adamant runs under reduced canvas, the crew working in the dim light of a single stern lantern. No other lights visible — no ships, no shore fires, just the stars and the phosphorescent glow of the bow wave.

Pell takes the first watch. You try to sleep in your cabin but the hammock swings with every swell and your mind won't stop working through the variables. Morrow. Ashford. The reef. The wreck. Briggs. The Red Correspondence. Too many players, too many unknowns.

Sometime after midnight you give up on sleep and go on deck. The stars are extraordinary — the Milky Way poured across the sky like spilled flour. The sea is black and calm.

choices.text: Watch the horizon until dawn

n_55.text:
The crew is in good order — lines coiled, deck scrubbed, the watch rotation running smoothly. You walk among them and catch fragments of conversation. The San Cristóbal. Crown gold. The Shallows.

They know enough to be worried and enough to be excited. The two feelings sit next to each other uneasily, the way they always do before a dangerous venture.

choices.text: Talk to Pell privately
Watch the sunset from the stern rail

n_56.text:
Pell is at the helm, one hand on the tiller, watching the compass. He glances at you when you approach.

"The crew's solid," he says before you ask. "They'll do what needs doing." A pause. "They like you, Captain. Not all of them — Henricks thinks you're reckless, and he's not entirely wrong — but most of them. That matters more than you think when things go bad."

He adjusts the tiller slightly. "What happened in Nassau? With Morrow. With the Council. With that skiff from the Crown ship." He shakes his head. "You don't have to tell me. But I sail better when I know what I'm sailing into."

choices.text: Tell Pell everything — Morrow's secret, Ashford's offer, all of it
"You know what you need to know, Pell."

n_57.text:
You tell him. All of it — Morrow's diver, the letter from the Red Correspondence, the three years of personal protection while Nassau went undefended. Ashford's offer. The ambush from Briggs's men.

Pell listens without interrupting. When you finish, he's quiet for a long time, his eyes on the horizon.

"Morrow kept herself safe while the rest of us sweated," he says finally. "I can't say I'm surprised. I'm not sure I wouldn't have done the same." He looks at you. "And Ashford's offer — safe passage for the Correspondence. That's a clean deal. Tempting."

"It is."

"But you're not going to take it." Not a question.

"I haven't decided."

Pell nods. "Fair enough. Whatever you decide, Captain, I'm on this ship. That's my decision, and it's already made."

choices.text: Watch the sunset from the stern rail

n_57a.text:
Pell nods. He's been a first mate long enough to know when not to push. "Aye, Captain. South-southeast it is."

He turns back to the compass. The conversation is over, but you can feel the gap it leaves — the things unsaid, the trust not quite extended. Pell will follow orders. He always does. But there's a difference between following orders and believing in the person giving them.

You leave him at the helm and go forward.

choices.text: Watch the sunset from the stern rail

n_58.text:
Dawn comes fast in these latitudes. The horizon goes from black to grey to gold in twenty minutes, and with the light comes the first sight of the Shallows.

The water is wrong. That's the only word for it. The deep blue of the open ocean gives way to bands of lighter colour — turquoise, jade, a sickly yellow-green where the coral comes within feet of the surface. The reef stretches across the horizon like a wound, white water breaking over submerged rock.

"The Shallows of Perdition," Pell says quietly. "I can see why they call it that."

Somewhere in that maze of coral and current, the San Cristóbal lies broken on the bottom. Your chart says she's there. The question is whether the Adamant can reach her.

choices.text: Check the horizon for other ships

n_59.text:
You raise the glass and scan the horizon. To the north, nothing — the open sea, empty. To the east, the low dark line of a cay, uninhabited. To the west, more reef.

To the south, a sail. Small, distant, hull-down. Too far to identify. It could be a fishing boat. It could be a Crown patrol. It could be Morrow.

Pell sees it too. "Company. Can't tell whose yet." He lowers his own glass. "We should get inside the reef before they get close enough to matter."

choices.text: Make for the eastern channel — Sable guides the way
Make for the eastern channel — you'll navigate it yourself

n_60.text:
Sable takes her position at the bow, one hand on the forestay, reading the water with eyes that have done this before. "Hard to port," she calls. "Now. Steady. Hold that line."

The Adamant enters the channel. The water changes colour beneath the hull — deep blue to turquoise to a clear, crystalline green where you can see the coral heads passing below like the ruins of a drowned city. Sable guides you through with calm precision, calling each turn before you can see the reason for it.

The channel narrows, widens, narrows again. A coral head scrapes the starboard hull with a sound like tearing cloth — but Sable has already called the correction, and the Adamant slides past with inches to spare.

Ten minutes. The longest ten minutes of your life. Then the channel opens into a broad, protected lagoon, and Sable steps back from the bow. "We're in."

choices.text: The Shallows of Perdition

n_60a.text:
You take the helm yourself, Morrow's chart spread on the binnacle, reading the water and the chart simultaneously. The eastern channel is there — you can see the deeper water, a darker line threading through the reef.

The Adamant enters the channel. Immediately the current grabs the hull and pushes you sideways. You correct, over-correct, feel the keel brush something solid beneath the waterline. The crew goes silent. Every man knows what that sound means.

test_luck.lucky_text: The coral releases the hull with a grinding shudder. The Adamant surges forward into deeper water, the channel widening ahead. You find the rhythm — read the chart, read the water, trust the tiller. Three more turns, each one tight enough to stop your heart, and then the channel opens into a broad lagoon. You're through. The keel is scored but intact.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The coral catches the hull and doesn't let go. The Adamant shudders, lists to port, and for one terrible moment you think she's going to break apart on the reef. Then the keel tears free with a sound like a cannon shot — planking splitting, water finding the gap. The crew scrambles to the pumps as you fight the Adamant through the last of the channel and into the lagoon beyond. She's leaking. Badly.

n_61.text:
The lagoon is beautiful and terrible. Crystal water over white sand, coral towers rising from the bottom like a cathedral's columns. Schools of fish move through the shallows in silver clouds. The air smells of salt and warm stone.

And somewhere below, in the deeper channel between two massive coral heads, the San Cristóbal lies in her grave. Ten years on the bottom. Her secrets intact. Her gold untouched.

The Adamant drops anchor. The chain rattles out, loud in the stillness. The crew gathers at the rail, looking down into water so clear they can see the anchor settle on the sand thirty feet below.

The Shallows of Perdition. You've arrived.

Pell turns to you. "Your orders, Captain."

choices.text: Begin the dive

n_62.text:
You take a last look at Nassau as the Adamant clears the harbour. The fort stands on its hill, the Union Jack hanging limp in the still air. Below it, the town — a jumble of rooftops and smoke and activity that looks, from this distance, almost peaceful. Almost normal.

But the four Crown ships tell the real story. Crown's Justice. Fervent. Resolve. Reliance. Ashford's patience made manifest in oak and iron. They sit at the harbour mouth like a sentence waiting to be finished.

Whatever you find at the Shallows — gold, secrets, the Red Correspondence — it comes back through those ships. That arithmetic hasn't changed.

choices.text: Turn your attention forward — to the sea ahead

n_63.text:
The harbour at midday is a study in contrasts. The civilian docks buzz with small activity — fishermen, chandlers, dock workers going about their business as if four warships weren't sitting in the harbour mouth. The naval anchorage, by contrast, is still. The Crown ships sit in formation, neat and threatening, their crews moving with drill-ground precision.

You walk past a warehouse where three men are arguing over the price of salt. Past a woman selling fried fish from a cart. Past two boys fishing off the dock with homemade lines. Nassau, going about the business of staying alive while its future hangs in the balance.

choices.text: A boy tugs at your sleeve
Continue walking the harbour

n_64.text:
Before you can leave the dock, movement in the alley behind the chandlery catches your eye. Two figures, moving too fast and too deliberately to be casual. One of them has a hand on something at his belt.

Pell sees them a second before you do. "Captain. Behind you."

They come out of the alley at a run. Briggs's men — you can tell by the desperation in their eyes. Minor pirates, hired muscle, the kind of men who take dangerous jobs because they can't get honest ones.

n_65.text:
The Adamant's hold is a study in organised scarcity. Water casks — enough for ten days. Provisions — seven days, maybe eight if you stretch them. Rope, spare canvas, carpentry tools. The supplies Gerda sold you are already stowed, adding another margin of safety.

Pell keeps a ledger of everything aboard, written in his cramped, precise hand. He shows you the numbers without comment. They are adequate, not generous. The blockade has made everything expensive and nothing abundant.

"We have enough," Pell says. "If we don't waste time at the Shallows. If nothing breaks that we can't fix. If the weather holds." He closes the ledger. "Three ifs. Not the worst odds I've sailed on."

choices.text: Go on deck

n_66.text:
The wind shifts as the Adamant makes her southward run. It comes around to the east — a fair wind, pushing you along at six knots. The sloop heels gently, her wake a white line on the dark water.

The crew settles into the rhythm of the passage. Watch changes, meals, the small repairs that are always needed on a working ship. A man sings — something low and rhythmic that the others pick up, a sea song about a ship that sailed to the edge of the world and found nothing there but more sea.

This is the Adamant at her best. A small ship, a capable crew, an open ocean. Whatever waits at the Shallows, this moment — the ship running free, the crew working together, the wind and the water and the sky — this moment is good.

choices.text: Continue south

n_67.text:
You check the horizon one more time before the light fails. The sail to the south is still there — closer now, but still hull-down. If it's Morrow, she's holding her distance. If it's a Crown patrol, they haven't seen you yet.

The last of the sunset bleeds out of the western sky. Stars appear — first one, then a handful, then thousands. The Adamant sails on through the dark, guided by Polaris and the chart and the instinct of a crew that has been doing this long enough to feel the sea in their bones.

choices.text: Rest before dawn

n_68.text:
You find the Broken Wheel open after all — the back door, not the front. The barkeep is mopping the floor with the resigned energy of someone who knows it will be dirty again by nightfall.

"Captain." He doesn't look up. "Morrow was asking about you. And a man named Briggs was in here last night, buying drinks for people who don't usually drink with him." The barkeep wrings out the mop. "Briggs is trying to put together a crew. He's been telling anyone who'll listen that the San Cristóbal is his claim."

Briggs. A name you keep hearing. A minor captain with major ambitions and no ship capable of running the blockade. Dangerous in the way that desperate men always are.

choices.text: Ask about Briggs
Leave the Broken Wheel — walk the harbour

n_69.text:
The barkeep sets down the mop and leans on the bar. "Briggs. Used to captain the Kestrel — a brig, decent ship. Tried to run the blockade eight days ago. Fervent put a shot across his bow and he turned back. Since then he's been drinking, scheming, and getting more dangerous by the day."

He wipes his hands on his apron. "He doesn't have a chart. He doesn't have a ship anymore — sold the Kestrel to pay his debts. What he has is a mouth and a grudge. He's been telling people you stole his claim." The barkeep shrugs. "Nobody believes him. But he's hiring men anyway. The kind of men who don't ask questions."

Briggs is a problem. Not a large one — not yet. But problems that go unaddressed have a way of growing.

choices.text: Leave the Broken Wheel — walk the harbour

n_70.text:
You check the Adamant's hull from the waterline, leaning over the dock to inspect the planking. She's sound — the copper sheathing is intact, the caulking tight, no sign of worm damage. A good ship. She'll handle the Shallows if you don't push her beyond what she can give.

A crewman named Tomás — your best diver — is working a splice nearby. He's a quiet man, dark-skinned, with the build of someone who has spent half his life underwater. He notices you looking.

"She'll float, Captain." He tests the splice and nods at it. "The question isn't whether the ship can make it. It's whether we can get down to thirty fathoms and back up again before the air runs out." He ties off the line. "I can do it. But I'll need calm water and no surprises."

choices.text: Return to the main deck

n_71.text:
At the harbour mouth, looking south through the gap between the Crown ships, you can just make out the colour of the water changing — the deep Atlantic blue giving way to something lighter on the horizon. The southern cays. The beginning of the Shallows.

A fisherman sits on the harbour wall nearby, mending a basket trap. He sees you looking south and shakes his head.

"Nothing good down there, Captain. The Shallows take more than they give." He holds up the trap — empty, broken. "Like everything else these days."

The blockade is squeezing Nassau dry. Every day the Crown ships sit there, the port gets a little poorer, a little more desperate. Whatever you find at the San Cristóbal, Nassau needs it. The question is whether Nassau will still be here when you get back.

choices.text: Return to the docks
